concepts.title: Conductivity and Resistivity
concepts.conceptBody:
Relation b/w Current Density and Electric Field

$\text{j}$ $~=\text{ne}{{\text{V}}_{\text{d}}}$

${{\text{V}}_{\text{d}}}$ $~=\left( \frac{\text{eE}}{\text{m}} \right)\tau $

$\Rightarrow $ $\text{j}=\frac{\text{n}{{\text{e}}^{2}}\text{E}\tau }{\text{m}}$

$\therefore $ $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ j}=\left( \frac{\text{n}{{\text{e}}^{2}}\tau }{\text{m}} \right)\text{E}=\sigma \text{E}$

where $\sigma =$ electric conductivity $=\left( \frac{\text{n}{{\text{e}}^{2}}}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ m}} \right)\tau $

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Resitivity }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }=\frac{1}{\sigma }=\frac{\text{m}}{\text{n}{{\text{e}}^{2}}\tau }$

concepts.title: Effect of Temperature on Resistivity
concepts.conceptBody:
Effect of Temperature on Resistivity  

$\rho =\frac{\text{m}}{\eta {{\text{e}}^{2}}\tau }$

$\text{n}\equiv $ number density $\equiv $ depends on nature of material

$\tau \equiv $ relaxation time $=$ depends on nature as well as temperature

In conductors : On increasing the temperature, the number of free electrons does not change (increase) appreciably, whereas the relaxation time decreases appreciably, (due to increase in ${{\text{V}}_{\text{rms }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ and decrease in mean free path due to increased amplitudes of vibrations. Hence, resistivity increases with increase in temperature.

In semi-conductors: On increasing the temperature, the resistivity of the semi-conductor decreases.

Reason- Inside a semi-conductor the number of free electrons increases appreciably with the increase in temperatures, whereas the relaxation time does not decrease appreciably and hence, the overall effect is the decrease in resistivity.

The number of free electrons in the semi-conductor varies with the temperature as:

$\text{n}={{\text{n}}_{0}}{{\text{e}}^{-\text{Eg}/\text{kT}}}$

where $\text{Eg}$ = is the difference valence of energy level between valence and conduction band.

In terms of electrical conductivity:

$\sigma ={{\sigma }_{0}}{{\text{e}}^{-\frac{\text{Eg}}{\text{kT}}}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\because \sigma =\eta \text{e}\mu $

At high temperature : $\text{T}\to \infty \left( \sigma ={{\sigma }_{0}} \right)$

The semi-conductor behaves as a conductor.

At low temperature : $\text{T}\to 0,\sigma =0$

The semi-conductor behaves as an insulator.

concepts.title: Resistance Part 1
concepts.conceptBody:
Resistance 

The electrical resistance of an object is a measure of its opposition to the passage of a steady electric current. An object of uniform cross section will have a resistance proportional to its length and inversely proportional to its cross-sectional area, and proportional to the resistivity of the material.

Electrical resistance shares some conceptual parallels with the mechanical notion of friction. The SI unit of electrical resistance is the ohm, symbol $\text{ }\!\!\Omega\!\!\text{ }$. Resistance's reciprocal quantity is electrical conductance measured in siemens, symbol $\text{S}$.

The resistance of a resistive object determines the amount of current through the object for a given potential difference across the object, in accordance with Ohm's law:

$\text{I}=\frac{\text{V}}{\text{R}}$

where,

$\text{R}$ is the resistance of the object, measured in ohm, equivalent to J.s/C ${{~}^{2}}$

$\text{V}$ is the potential difference across the object, measured in volts.

I is the current through the object, measured in amperes.

For a wide variety of materials and conditions, the electrical resistance does not depend on the amount of current through or the amount of voltage across the object, meaning that the resistance $R$ is constant for the given temperature. Therefore, the resistance of an object can be defined as the ratio of voltage to current:

$\text{R}=\frac{\text{V}}{\text{I}}$

The resistance $R$ of a conductor of uniform cross section can be computed as

$\text{R}=\frac{\rho \text{l}}{\text{A}}$

where,

$l$ is the length of the conductor, measured in meters.

A is the cross-sectional area, measured in square meters.

$\rho $ is the electrical resistivity (also called specific electrical resistance) of the material, measured in Ohm meter. Resistivity is a measure of the material's ability to oppose electric current.

concepts.title: Resistance Part 2
concepts.conceptBody:
Resistivity

Electrical resistivity (also known as specific electrical resistance or volume resistivity) is a measure of how strongly a material opposes the flow of electric current. A low resistivity indicates a material that readily allows the movement of electrical charge. The SI unit of electrical resistivity is the ohm meter $\left( \text{ }\!\!\Omega\!\!\text{ m} \right)$.

A piece of resistive material with electrical contacts on both ends.

Electrical resistivity $\rho $ (Greek : rho) is defined by,

$\rho =\frac{E}{J}$

where,

$\rho $ is the static resistivity (measured in volts metres per ampere, $\text{Vm}/\text{A}$ );

$\text{E}$ is the magnitude of the electric field (measured in volts per metre, $\text{V}/\text{m}$ );

$\text{J}$ is the magnitude of the current density (measured in amperes per square metre, $\text{A}/{{\text{m}}^{2}}$ ).

The electrical resistivity $\rho $ can also be given by,

$\rho =\text{R}\frac{\text{A}}{\ell }$

where

$\rho $ is the static resistivity (measured on ohm-metres, $\text{ }\!\!\Omega\!\!\text{ m}$ );

$\text{R}$ is the electrical resistance of a uniform specimen of the material (measured in ohm, $\text{ }\!\!\Omega\!\!\text{ }$ ) ;

$\ell $ is the length of the piece of material (measured in metres, $m$ ) :

$\text{A}$ is the cross-sectional area of the specimen (measured in square metres, ${{\text{m}}^{2}}$ ).

Finally, electrical resistivity is also defined as the inverse of the conductivity $\sigma $ (sigma), of the material, or

$\rho =\frac{1}{\sigma }$

concepts.title: Temperature Coefficient of Resistivity
concepts.conceptBody:
TEMPERATURE COEFFICIENT OF RESISTIVITY $\left( \alpha \right)$ 

The fractional change in resistivity for unit $1\text{C}$ or $1\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ K}$ change of temperature is called temperature coefficient of resistivity $\left( \alpha \right)$.

$\alpha $ $~=\left( \frac{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }\rho }{\rho \text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }\theta } \right)\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ (Average }\!\!~\!\!\text{ value) }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

$\therefore $ $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\rho ={{\rho }_{0}}\left( 1+\alpha \text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }\theta \right)\Rightarrow R=$ ${{\text{R}}_{0}}\left( 1+\alpha \text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }\theta \right)$

$\alpha $ $~=\left( \frac{\text{d}\rho }{\rho \text{d}\theta } \right)\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ (Absolute }\!\!~\!\!\text{ value) }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

$\therefore $ $~\mathop{\int }_{{{\rho }_{0}}}^{\rho }\frac{\text{d}\rho }{\rho }=\mathop{\int }_{{{\theta }_{0}}}^{\theta }\alpha \text{d}\theta $

$\text{ln}\left( \frac{\rho }{{{\rho }_{0}}} \right)$ $~=\mathop{\int }_{{{\theta }_{0}}}^{\theta }\alpha \text{d}\theta $

$\alpha $ is + ve for a conductor.

$\alpha $ is -ve for a semi-conductor.

In general,

$\rho ={{\rho }_{0}}\left[ 1+\alpha \text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }\theta +\beta \text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }{{\theta }^{2}}+\gamma \delta {{\theta }^{3}}+\ldots \ldots . \right]$

but for small temperature change, higher powers are neglected.

Variation of $\rho $ : 

Super-conductor: A substance whose resistivity becomes zero at temperature critical temperature is called super-conductor.
